title: Ubuntu Terminal Networking Commands
description: A practical Ubuntu terminal quiz focused on non-basic commands, especially networking tools and system inspection commands. Includes typed-answer versions of all multiple-choice questions.

questions.prompt: What does the command `ping example.com` mainly test?

options.label: Whether a host responds over the network
options.explanation: ping sends ICMP echo requests to test basic reachability and latency.

questions.explanation: ping sends ICMP echo requests to test basic reachability and latency.

questions.prompt: What does the command `ping example.com` mainly test? Answer in your own words.
questions.answer: Whether a host responds over the network
questions.explanation: ping sends ICMP echo requests to test basic reachability and latency.

questions.prompt: Which command shows IP addresses assigned to network interfaces on modern Ubuntu?

options.label: ip addr
options.explanation: `ip addr` shows addresses and interface details using the modern iproute2 toolset.

questions.explanation: `ip addr` shows addresses and interface details using the modern iproute2 toolset.

questions.prompt: Type the command shows IP addresses assigned to network interfaces on modern Ubuntu.
questions.explanation: `ip addr` shows addresses and interface details using the modern iproute2 toolset.

questions.prompt: In `ip addr`, what does the state `UP` usually mean for an interface?

options.label: The interface is enabled
options.explanation: `UP` means the interface is administratively enabled and available for networking.

questions.explanation: `UP` means the interface is administratively enabled and available for networking.

questions.prompt: In `ip addr`, what does the state `UP` usually mean for an interface? Type the correct answer.
questions.explanation: `UP` means the interface is administratively enabled and available for networking.

questions.explanation: `ip route` displays the kernel routing table, including the default route.

questions.prompt: What does `default via 192.168.1.1` mean in routing output?

options.label: Traffic without a more specific route goes through 192.168.1.1
options.explanation: The default route is used when no more specific network route matches the destination.

questions.explanation: The default route is used when no more specific network route matches the destination.

questions.answer: Traffic without a more specific route goes through 192.168.1.1
questions.explanation: The default route is used when no more specific network route matches the destination.

questions.prompt: Which command creates a dynamic SOCKS proxy on local port 1080?

questions.prompt: What is the difference between SSH local `-L` and remote `-R` forwarding?

options.label: -L opens a local listening port; -R opens a listening port on the remote side
options.explanation: Local forwarding listens locally, while remote forwarding listens on the remote server side.

questions.explanation: Local forwarding listens locally, while remote forwarding listens on the remote server side.

questions.prompt: What is the main risk of `sudo fuser -k 3000/tcp`?

options.label: It kills processes using that port, possibly stopping an important service
options.explanation: The command terminates matching processes and can disrupt running applications.

options.label: It removes the package that owns the process

reload only shows logs; restart only shows status

questions.prompt: What does `chmod 640 file` allow?

options.label: Owner read/write, group read, others no access
options.explanation: Mode 640 means owner can read/write, group can read, and others have no permissions.

options.label: Owner execute only, group write only, others read
Owner read only, group execute, others write

options.label: It can change many files and break security or services
